people.name: The Báb (Mírzá 'Alí-Muhammad)
people.bio:
The Báb (meaning 'The Gate') was the Prophet-Herald of the Bahá'í Revelation. Born in Shiraz as Siyyid 'Alí-Muhammad, He declared His mission in 1844, initiating a spiritual revolution that shook 19th-century Persia. He called for moral regeneration and prepared His followers for the imminent arrival of 'Him Whom God shall make manifest' (Bahá'u'lláh).

His teachings spread rapidly, provoking intense opposition from the Islamic clergy and the royal court. He was imprisoned in the remote mountain fortresses of Máh-Kú and Chihríq, and was ultimately martyred by a firing squad of 750 soldiers in Tabriz on July 9, 1850.
people.confusionAlert: Sometimes referred to by His birth name Mírzá 'Alí-Muhammad. Do not confuse with other individuals named Mírzá 'Alí or Muhammad.

people.name: Bahá'u'lláh (Mírzá Husayn-'Alí)
people.bio:
Bahá'u'lláh (born Mírzá Husayn-'Alí in Tehran) was a nobleman who rejected a life of courtly luxury to champion the Báb's cause. He became one of the most prominent Bábís, organizing the historic Conference of Badasht where the old laws of Islam were formally abrogated. He suffered imprisonment, torture, and the loss of all His properties following the attempt on the Shah's life in 1852, during which He was cast into the infamous subterranean prison, the Siyáh-Chál.

It was in the darkness of the Siyáh-Chál that He received the first intimations of His own divine mission. He is the Promised One foretold by the Báb and all previous scriptures, whose teachings form the foundation of the Bahá'í Faith.
people.confusionAlert: In Nabíl's Narrative, He is primarily referred to by His birth name Mírzá Husayn-'Alí or Jináb-i-Bahá. Do not confuse with Mullá Husayn.

people.name: Mullá Husayn-i-Bushrú'í

relations.type: First Letter of the Living, Disciple of

people.bio:
Mullá Husayn was the first Letter of the Living and the initial believer in the Báb. A brilliant young scholar under Siyyid Kázim, he spent forty days in prayer and fasting before traveling to Shiraz, where on May 23, 1844, he recognized the Báb's station. For this, he was given the title 'Bábu'l-Báb' (the Gate of the Gate).

He was a figure of legendary courage and physical strength. He walked on foot across Persia to spread the new teachings, and later raised the Black Standard of Khurasan, marching with a band of Bábís to the forests of Mazandaran. He oversaw the construction of Fort Shaykh Tabarsí and led the defense against royal armies, performing feats of defense before being martyred by a bullet during a night sortie in early 1849.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Bahá'u'lláh (Mírzá Husayn-'Alí) or the Imam Husayn (the Shia martyr whose shrine is in Karbila).

people.name: Quddús (Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Bárfurúshí)

relations.type: Last Letter of the Living, Companion on Pilgrimage

people.bio:
Quddús was the 18th and final Letter of the Living, considered by the Báb to have the highest spiritual rank among His disciples. Born Mullá Muhammad-'Alí in Mazandaran, he accompanied the Báb on His pilgrimage to Mecca and Medina, where he courageously assisted in proclaiming the Faith. Upon returning, he was arrested and tortured in Shiraz.

He was the spiritual leader of the Bábís at Fort Shaykh Tabarsí, where his presence inspired the defenders to withstand armies for months. After the fall of the fort due to a treacherous peace treaty, Quddús was delivered to the hostile clergy of Bárfurúsh, who tortured him brutally and martyred him in the public square in May 1849.
people.confusionAlert: His birth name was Mullá Muhammad-'Alí. Do not confuse with Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Zanjání (Hujjat) or Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Zunúzí (Anís).

people.name: Vahíd (Siyyid Yahyá-i-Dárábí)
people.bio:
Vahíd (born Siyyid Yahyá-i-Dárábí) was one of the most learned religious figures in Persia. He possessed such a prodigious memory that he had memorized over 30,000 Islamic traditions. Muhammad Shah sent him to investigate the Báb's claims, expecting him to disprove them. Instead, during their interviews in Shiraz, Vahíd was overwhelmed by the Báb's divine knowledge and became a devoted follower.

He traveled across Persia, converting thousands. When persecutions intensified, he led the Bábí defense during the Nayríz Upheaval, holding out in the fort of Khájih. He was martyred in 1850 after the townspeople violated an oath sworn on the Qur'án to grant the Bábís peace.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Mírzá Yahyá (Subh-i-Azal), Bahá'u'lláh's half-brother.

people.name: Hujjat (Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Zanjání)
people.bio:
Hujjat (born Mullá Muhammad-'Alí) was an influential and independent-minded Mujtahid of Zanjan. Known for his theological brilliance and refusal to follow blind religious conformity, he accepted the Báb after receiving a written text from Him through a messenger.

His conversion prompted nearly his entire congregation to become Bábís, which enraged the local clergy. The resulting tensions erupted into the Zanján Upheaval (1850–1851), where Hujjat led several thousand Bábís (including women and children) in defending their quarter against imperial troops. Hujjat was wounded by a cannonball and died shortly before the fort fell. His wife and children were also martyred.
people.confusionAlert: His birth name was Mullá Muhammad-'Alí. Do not confuse with Quddús or Anís.

people.name: Táhirih (Qurratu'l-'Ayn)

relations.type: Protected by and closely allied with

people.bio:
Táhirih (born Fátimih Baraghání) was a poet, theologian, and the only female Letter of the Living. Her intellect was so formidable that she was permitted to attend the lectures of Siyyid Kázim behind a curtain and debate male scholars. She recognized the Báb through a dream and letter, and was accepted as a disciple without meeting Him in person.

She is famous for her role at the Conference of Badasht, where she appeared in public without a veil, symbolizing the separation of the Bábí Dispensation from Islamic tradition. After years of house arrest, she was choked to death with a silk scarf in a Tehran garden in August 1852. Her famous final words were: 'You can kill me as soon as you like, but you cannot stop the emancipation of women.'
people.confusionAlert: Sometimes referred to as Qurratu'l-'Ayn (given by Siyyid Kázim) or Táhirih (given by Bahá'u'lláh at Badasht).

people.name: Shaykh Ahmad-i-Ahsá'í
people.bio:
Shaykh Ahmad-i-Ahsá'í was the founder of the Shaykhí school of Shia Islam. He arose in the early 19th century to protest the corruption of the clergy and teach that the arrival of the Promised Mihdí (the Báb) and the Return of Christ (Bahá'u'lláh) were imminent. He explained Shia prophecies symbolically, which drew both massive devotion and accusations of heresy from orthodox Mujtahids.

He spent his final years traveling through Persia and Iraq, sowing the seeds of expectation. He instructed his students, most notably Siyyid Kázim, to disperse and search for the Promised One upon his death. He died in 1826 while on a pilgrimage to Mecca.
people.confusionAlert: Mainly referred to as Shaykh Ahmad. Do not confuse with Mírzá Ahmad-i-Kátib.

people.name: Siyyid Kázim-i-Rashtí
people.bio:
Siyyid Kázim-i-Rashtí was the designated successor of Shaykh Ahmad. He led the Shaykhí community from Karbilá, defending the teachings against orthodox attacks. He focused intensely on preparing his disciples to recognize the Twin Revelations, telling them that the Promised One was already alive among them.

He refused to name a successor, telling his students that upon his death they must leave their books, homes, and studies, and wander the earth to search for the Mihdí. He died in late 1843, shortly before the Báb declared His mission in May 1844.
people.confusionAlert: Mainly referred to as Siyyid Kázim. Do not confuse with other individuals named Kázim.

people.name: Mírzá Yahyá (Subh-i-Azal)

relations.type: Nominal head of Bábís appointed by

people.bio:
Mírzá Yahyá was the younger half-brother of Bahá'u'lláh. Following the martyrdom of the Báb, he was appointed as the nominal head of the Bábí community as a protective measure to shield Bahá'u'lláh from state scrutiny. However, he spent most of his time in hiding and disguise, refusing to lead in times of danger.

Following the attempt on the Shah's life in 1852, he fled to Baghdad in disguise. He later opposed Bahá'u'lláh when He publicly declared His mission, claiming leadership for himself and creating a schism among the Bábís. He is remembered as the primary Covenant-breaker of the Báb Dispensation.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Vahíd (Siyyid Yahyá-i-Dárábí) or Yahyá Khán (the warden of Chihríq).

people.name: Mírzá Músá (Áqáy-i-Kalím)
people.bio:
Mírzá Músá, commonly known as Áqáy-i-Kalím, was one of the two faithful brothers of Bahá'u'lláh (the other being Mírzá Mihdí, who died young). He accepted the Báb's cause early on and stood resolutely beside Bahá'u'lláh through all His exiles, imprisonments, and hardships.

He was recognized by Shoghi Effendi as one of the Apostles of Bahá'u'lláh, serving as a trusted pillar of the community and assisting in managing the affairs of the exiles in Baghdad, Adrianople, and Acre.
people.confusionAlert: Often called Kalím in the narrative. Do not confuse with other Mirzas.

people.name: Hájí Mírzá Áqásí

relations.type: Prime Minister and spiritual tutor of

people.bio:
Hájí Mírzá Áqásí was the Grand Vizier (Prime Minister) of Persia during the reign of Muhammad Shah. He exerted immense, almost hypnotic spiritual and political influence over the weak-willed Shah. Fearing that the Báb's growing popularity would strip him of his power, he actively blocked the Báb from meeting the Shah.

He ordered the Báb's confinement in the remote mountain fortresses of Máh-Kú and Chihríq, attempting to isolate Him from His followers. After the death of Muhammad Shah in 1848, he fell from power, was pillaged by his enemies, and fled to Iraq, where he died in obscurity.
people.confusionAlert: Often referred to simply as Áqásí or the Grand Vizier. Do not confuse with other Hajis.

people.name: Muhammad Sháh

relations.type: Sovereign who ordered the arrest of

people.bio:
Muhammad Sháh was the king (Sháh) of Persia from 1834 to 1848. A weak ruler plagued by illness, he fell entirely under the sway of his Grand Vizier, Hájí Mírzá Áqásí. Intrigued by the reports of the Báb, he sent Siyyid Yahyá-i-Dárábí (Vahíd) to investigate Him.

Although Vahíd reported back that the Báb was the promised Savior, the Shah was persuaded by the Grand Vizier to avoid meeting Him and instead order His confinement in Máh-Kú. Muhammad Shah died of gout in September 1848, sparking civil unrest and immediate upheavals across Persia.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with the Báb (Mírzá 'Alí-Muhammad) or other figures named Muhammad.

people.name: Násiri'd-Dín Sháh
people.bio:
Násiri'd-Dín Sháh was the Crown Prince (Násiri'd-Dín Mírzá) who ascended the throne of Persia in 1848 after his father's death, reigning until his assassination in 1896. During the Báb's trial in Tabriz in 1848, the young prince presided over the assembly where the Báb declared His station to the assembled clergy.

Upon becoming Shah, under the guidance of his prime minister Amír Kabír, he ordered the execution of the Báb in 1850 and oversaw the brutal persecution of thousands of Bábís, culminating in the horrific massacres of 1852 following the failed assassination attempt on his life.
people.confusionAlert: Called Násiri'd-Dín Mírzá when he was the Crown Prince. Do not confuse with his father Muhammad Shah.

people.name: Mullá 'Alíy-i-Bastámí
titles: First Martyr of the Bábí Dispensation

relations.type: Second Letter of the Living, Disciple of

people.bio:
Mullá 'Alíy-i-Bastámí was the second Letter of the Living and the first martyr of the Bábí Dispensation. He accepted the Báb immediately after Mullá Husayn. The Báb sent him to Iraq to announce the declaration to the leading Shia clerics of Karbilá and Najaf.

His bold proclamations provoked a massive backlash from both Shia and Sunni authorities. He was arrested, tried in Baghdad in a famous inter-sectarian trial, and sent to Istanbul as a prisoner, where he was condemned to hard labor. He died in captivity around 1846, becoming the first to lay down his life for the Báb's cause.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Siyyid 'Alí-i-Zunúzí or other individuals named 'Alí.

people.name: Anís (Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Zunúzí)
people.bio:
Anís (born Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Zunúzí) was a young Bábí from Tabriz who achieved eternal fame for his absolute devotion to the Báb. When the Báb was brought to Tabriz for execution, Anís threw himself at the Báb's feet, pleading to be allowed to share His martyrdom.

On July 9, 1850, he was suspended alongside the Báb in the public square. When the first regiment of 750 soldiers fired, the smoke cleared to reveal the Báb unharmed and the ropes severed, but Anís stood there untouched, refusing to leave the Báb's side. He was suspended again and shot by the second regiment, his body mingled with the Báb's in death.
people.confusionAlert: Birth name was Mullá Muhammad-'Alí. The name Anís (meaning 'Companion') was given to him by the Báb. Do not confuse with Quddús or Hujjat.

people.name: Hájí Siyyid Javád-i-Karbilá'í
people.bio:
Hájí Siyyid Javád-i-Karbilá'í was a revered and elder scholar of the Shaykhí school who had been a close associate of both Shaykh Ahmad and Siyyid Kázim. He recognized the Báb's station immediately after His declaration.

He was renowned for his saintly life and traveled extensively, helping to confirm many prominent figures (including Vahíd) in their faith. He survived the early upheavals and later became a devoted follower of Bahá'u'lláh.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other Siyyids or Mirzas.

people.name: Siyyid 'Alí-i-Zunúzí
people.bio:
Siyyid 'Alí-i-Zunúzí was a prominent and wealthy merchant of Tabriz who accepted the Báb. When his step-son Anís (Mullá Muhammad-'Alí) became obsessed with the Báb, Siyyid 'Alí tried to protect him by locking him in his room to prevent his arrest.

Despite his efforts, Anís escaped to join the Báb during His final days in Tabriz. Siyyid 'Alí is remembered as a devoted and sorrowful figure who witnessed the martyrdom of his step-son.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with the Báb (Mírzá 'Alí-Muhammad) or Mullá 'Alíy-i-Bastámí.

people.name: Mullá Yusúf-i-Ardibilí

relations.type: Letter of the Living, Disciple of

people.bio:
Mullá Yusúf-i-Ardibilí was a Letter of the Living. He was a learned divine who joined the Bábís at Fort Shaykh Tabarsí, participating actively in the defense and proving himself to be a capable and spiritual soldier.

He was captured after the fall of the fort and was martyred alongside the other survivors by the royal troops in Mazandaran in 1849.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other Mullas.

people.name: Mullá Mihdíy-i-Yazdí
people.bio:
Mullá Mihdíy-i-Yazdí was a prominent Bábí of Yazd. When Vahíd arrived in Yazd to proclaim the Báb's message, Mullá Mihdí hosted him in his home and became one of his most active supporters.

He participated in defending Vahíd when local mobs and government forces attacked them, and was eventually arrested and martyred during the persecutions that swept Yazd.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other figures named Mihdí.

people.name: Mírzá Ahmad-i-Kátib
people.bio:
Mírzá Ahmad-i-Kátib (also known as Mírzá Ahmad-i-Qazvíní) was the close amanuensis (secretary) of the Báb. He transcribed many of the Báb's writings during His imprisonment and helped copy and distribute them to the believers.

He was highly regarded for his beautiful calligraphy and deep devotion. He traveled between Shiraz, Tehran, and the northern fortresses to convey the Báb's messages. He was martyred during the massacres of 1852 in Tehran.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Shaykh Ahmad-i-Ahsá'í.

people.name: Hájí Mírzá Siyyid 'Alí (Khál-i-A'zam)
people.bio:
Hájí Mírzá Siyyid 'Alí, known as Khál-i-A'zam, was the maternal uncle of the Báb who raised Him after His father died. He was a respected merchant in Shiraz who initially protected the Báb out of family affection and later became a devoted believer.

He refused to recant his faith when arrested, choosing instead to share the fate of his Nephew. He was martyred in Tehran in 1850 as one of the Seven Martyrs of Tehran.
people.confusionAlert: Mainly referred to as Khál-i-A'zam. Do not confuse with other Siyyids.

people.name: Mullá Sádiq-i-Muqaddas (Jináb-i-Muqaddas)

relations.type: Early believer and victim of scourging under

people.bio:
Mullá Sádiq-i-Muqaddas was a prominent scholar who accepted the Báb. He was the first Bábí to add the name of the Báb to the traditional call to prayer (Adhán) in Shiraz, which led to his immediate arrest, scourging, and expulsion from the city.

He survived the early persecutions and was titled 'Muqaddas' (The Holy) by Bahá'u'lláh for his absolute devotion and service to the Faith.
people.confusionAlert: Often called Jináb-i-Muqaddas. Do not confuse with other Mullas.

people.name: Siyyid Basír-i-Hindí

relations.type: Believer who recognized Him by voice

people.bio:
Siyyid Basír-i-Hindí was a blind Indian nobleman and spiritual seeker of Shia descent. He traveled to Persia and recognized the Báb's station immediately upon hearing His voice. Despite his blindness, he possessed a profound spiritual sight and courage.

He traveled across Persia, proclaiming the Báb's message. He was arrested, tortured with extreme cruelty, and martyred in Tehran around 1851.
people.confusionAlert: Mainly called Siyyid Basír. Do not confuse with other Shia Siyyids.

people.name: Mullá Báqir-i-Tabrízí

relations.type: Letter of the Living, Disciple of

people.bio: Mullá Báqir-i-Tabrízí was a Letter of the Living. He was the only Letter of the Living who survived all the Bábí upheavals and lived to recognize Bahá'u'lláh's station in Baghdad, remaining a faithful follower until his death.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other Mullas.

people.name: Sulaymán Khán
people.bio:
Sulaymán Khán was a nobleman and military official who became a devoted Bábí. He is famous for the extraordinary manner of his martyrdom in Tehran in August 1852. The executioners cut holes in his chest and shoulders and inserted burning candles into his wounds.

He walked to the place of execution singing verses of joy, blowing out the candles himself as they burned down. His martyrdom is recorded as one of the most heroic and moving events in early Bábí history.
people.confusionAlert: Mainly called Sulaymán Khán. Do not confuse with other Khans.

people.name: Mullá Sálih-i-Baraghání
people.bio:
Mullá Sálih-i-Baraghání was a prominent and highly respected Mujtahid of Qazvín and the father of Táhirih. He loved his daughter deeply and educated her in theology and jurisprudence, but was deeply troubled by her conversion to the Bábí Faith.

He spent his final years in grief, torn between his love for his daughter and his orthodox religious obligations.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with his brother Mullá Muhammad-Taqí.

people.name: Mullá Muhammad-Taqí-i-Baraghání
people.bio:
Mullá Muhammad-Taqí-i-Baraghání (known in Bahá'í history as the Third Martyr) was a fanatical Shia Mujtahid of Qazvín who vehemently denounced Shaykh Ahmad and Siyyid Kázim, and later the Báb.

His denouncements provoked a young Bábí to attack and kill him while he was praying in the mosque in 1847. His murder sparked a wave of arrests and persecutions of Bábís across Qazvín and Tehran, including the imprisonment of Táhirih.
people.confusionAlert: Often called Mullá Taqí. Do not confuse with his brother Mullá Sálih.

people.name: Mullá 'Abdu'r-Rahím
people.bio: Mullá 'Abdu'r-Rahím was the chief Mujtahid of Zanján who led the orthodox opposition against Hujjat. He wrote numerous letters to the Shah and the Grand Vizier denouncing Hujjat as a heretic, which ultimately led to Hujjat's arrest and the subsequent Zanján Upheaval.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other Mullas.

people.name: Hamzih Mírzá
people.bio:
Hamzih Mírzá, titled Hishmatu'd-Dawlih, was a brother of Muhammad Shah and a prominent prince of the royal Qajar house. He was appointed as the military commander of the imperial forces sent to crush the Bábí defenders at Fort Shaykh Tabarsí.

He suffered several humiliating defeats at the hands of Mullá Husayn's small band of defenders before ultimately besieging the fort and capturing it through a treacherous oath of peace.
people.confusionAlert: Often referred to as Hishmatu'd-Dawlih. Do not confuse with other Mirzas.

people.name: 'Alí Khán of Máh-Kú
people.bio:
'Alí Khán was the warden of the mountain fortress of Máh-Kú where the Báb was imprisoned. Initially hostile and strict, he was deeply affected by the Báb's saintly character, His nightly prayers, and the devotion of His followers.

He eventually relaxed the prison conditions, allowing pilgrims to visit the Báb, and became a secret believer, refusing to carry out the harsher orders of the Grand Vizier.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other Khans or individuals named 'Alí.

people.name: Yahyá Khán
people.bio: Yahyá Khán was the warden of the fortress of Chihríq and the brother-in-law of the Grand Vizier, Hájí Mírzá Áqásí. Although he was expected to enforce strict confinement on the Báb, he also fell under the spell of the Báb's character and treated Him with kindness and respect.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Vahíd (Siyyid Yahyá) or Mírzá Yahyá (Subh-i-Azal).

events.title: Declaration of the Báb
events.description: On May 23, 1844, Siyyid 'Alí-Muhammad declared His mission as the Báb to Mullá Husayn in Shiraz, marking the birth of the Bábí Faith.

events.title: Arrest and Scourging of Mullá Sádiq
events.description: Mullá Sádiq-i-Muqaddas was arrested and publicly scourged in Shiraz for adding the Báb's name to the call to prayer.

events.title: House Arrest of the Báb
events.description: The Báb was arrested upon His return from pilgrimage and placed under house arrest at the home of His uncle, Khál-i-A'zam.

events.title: Unveiling at Badasht Preparation
events.description: Tehran served as the assembly point for many early Bábís, where Bahá'u'lláh organized the Bábís and prepared them for the conference.

events.title: Martyrdom of the Seven Martyrs
events.description: Seven prominent Bábís, including the Báb's uncle Khál-i-A'zam, were publicly executed in Tehran for refusing to recant their faith.

events.title: Imprisonment in the Siyáh-Chál
events.description: Following the attempt on the Shah's life in 1852, Bahá'u'lláh and other Bábís were cast into the dark dungeon, where Bahá'u'lláh received His divine revelation.

events.title: Proclamation by Vahíd
events.description: Vahíd arrived in Yazd, attracting thousands to the Báb's cause and hosting massive religious debates.

events.title: Anti-Bábí Riots
events.description: The local clergy incited mobs to attack Vahíd and his hosts, resulting in the martyrdom of several early believers and forcing Vahíd to flee to Nayríz.

events.title: Trial of the Báb
events.description: The Báb was brought from His fortress prison to stand trial before a tribunal of Shia clergy and Crown Prince Násiri'd-Dín Mírzá, where He declared His station.

events.title: Martyrdom of the Báb
events.description: On July 9, 1850, the Báb and His disciple Anís were executed in the public square of Tabriz by a firing squad.

events.title: Refuge under Manúchihr Khán
events.description: The Báb was hosted in Isfahan by the sympathetic governor Manúchihr Khán, who offered Him protection and his entire fortune to spread the Faith.

events.title: Governor's Death and Arrest
events.description: Upon the governor's death in 1847, the Báb was arrested by order of the Grand Vizier and escorted to Tehran and then Máh-Kú.

events.title: Conversion of Hujjat
events.description: Hujjat converted to the Bábí Faith, leading his entire congregation of several thousand to accept the Báb.

events.title: The Zanján Upheaval
events.description: A brutal siege (1850-1851) where the Bábí quarter, led by Hujjat, defended itself against imperial troops before being massacred.

events.title: Arrival of Vahíd
events.description: Vahíd arrived in Nayriz, leading to the conversion of the local governor's family and thousands of townspeople.

events.title: Nayríz Upheavals
events.description: A series of battles at the fort of Khájih where Vahíd and the Bábís defended themselves before being betrayed by a false treaty.

events.title: Conference of Badasht
events.description: A historic gathering of 81 Bábís in 1848, organized by Bahá'u'lláh, where Táhirih appeared unveiled, signaling the independence of the new dispensation.

events.title: Imprisonment of the Báb
events.description: The Báb was imprisoned in the castle of Maku, where He composed the Bayán, His primary doctrinal work.

events.title: Conversion of warden 'Alí Khán
events.description: The warden 'Alí Khán fell under the Báb's influence and relaxed the prison restrictions, allowing pilgrims to reach the Báb.

events.title: Confinement of the Báb
events.description: The Báb was transferred to Chihríq fortress to isolate Him further from His followers, but the warden also became sympathetic.

events.title: Shaykhí Lectures
events.description: Siyyid Kázim taught the imminent advent of the Mihdí, making Karbila the focal point of spiritual search.

events.title: Arrest of Bastámí
events.description: Mullá 'Alíy-i-Bastámí arrived to announce the Báb's declaration, leading to his arrest and the start of official persecutions.

events.title: Shaykh Ahmad debates
events.description: Shaykh Ahmad resided and taught in Najaf, establishing his intellectual authority.

events.title: Arrest and Martyrdom of Quddús
events.description: Quddús was brutally tortured and martyred in the public square of his home city by an incited mob led by local clerics.

events.title: Birthplace of Mullá Husayn
events.description: The hometown of Mullá Husayn, from which he set out to find the Promised One.

events.title: Murder of Mullá Taqí
events.description: The uncle and father-in-law of Táhirih was murdered in the mosque, sparking massive arrests of Bábís.

events.title: House arrest of Táhirih
events.description: Táhirih was imprisoned in her family home before being secretly rescued by agents of Bahá'u'lláh and brought to Tehran.

events.title: Raising the Black Standard
events.description: Mullá Husayn raised the Black Standard of Khurasan in Mashhad, marching with Bábí defenders toward Mazandaran.
